pattern: Each pig is a base-(rounds+1) digit, since the round it dies in (or never) encodes one symbol; the minimum pig count is the smallest exponent making that base raised to it cover every bucket.
hints: Think about how much information a single pig gives you: across the available testing rounds it can die in one of several rounds, or survive, and that set of outcomes is its alphabet.
With T total rounds a pig has T+1 distinguishable outcomes, so p pigs jointly produce (T+1)^p distinct outcome combinations, one of which must identify each bucket.
Compute the number of rounds as minutesToTest divided by minutesToDie, then find the least p with (rounds + 1) raised to the p at least equal to buckets.

edge_inputs.description: one testing round, four buckets needs two pigs
two rounds available with the same four buckets still needs two pigs
a single bucket needs no pigs at all
exactly base-squared buckets sits on the boundary and still fits two pigs
one over a perfect power forces an extra pig
many rounds shrink the pig count dramatically
two buckets with one round needs a single pig

stress.description: random valid triples with minutesToDie no greater than minutesToTest
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    buckets = rng.randint(1, 1000)
    die = rng.randint(1, 100)
    test = rng.randint(die, 100)
    return [buckets, die, test]
stress.note: size is irrelevant; the inputs are three scalars within constraints

stress.description: stress the perfect-power boundaries where rounding decisions matter
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    die = rng.randint(1, 50)
    test = rng.randint(die, 100)
    rounds = test // die
    base = rounds + 1
    p = rng.randint(0, 3)
    val = base ** p
    buckets = max(1, min(1000, val + rng.randint(-1, 1)))
    return [buckets, die, test]
stress.note: constructs bucket counts near exact powers of the base
